vulnerability.title: Ensure SELinux State is Enforcing
vulnerability.description: The SELinux state should be set to at system boot time. In the file /etc/selinux/config, add or correct the following line to configure the system to boot into enforcing mode: SELINUX= Ensure that all files have correct SELinux labels by running: fixfiles onboot Then reboot the system.
vulnerability.recommendation: Remediation script: # Remediation is applicable only in certain platforms if rpm --quiet -q kernel-core; then var_selinux_state='' if [ -e "/etc/selinux/config" ] ; then LC_ALL=C sed -i "/^SELINUX=/Id" "/etc/selinux/config" else touch "/etc/selinux/config" fi # make sure file has newline at the end sed -i -e '$a\' "/etc/selinux/config" cp "/etc/selinux/config" "/etc/selinux/config.bak" # Insert at the end of the file printf '%s\n' "SELINUX=$var_selinux_state" >> "/etc/selinux/config" # Clean up after ourselves. rm "/etc/selinux/config.bak" fixfiles onboot else >&2 echo 'Remediation is not applicable, nothing was done' fi

triage_decision.reason: The remediation only edits /etc/selinux/config and runs the standard fixfiles relabeling, both of which are common STIG actions. While enforcing SELinux can cause services to fail if policies are incomplete, the fix includes a relabel step and a reboot, minimizing the chance of immediate lockout. Therefore it is considered safe to remediate automatically.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; Potential service disruption if SELinux policies are not fully compatible; Temporary loss of functionality until reboot completes

execution_details.stderr: Warning: your password will expire in 3 days.

execution_details.stdout:
# This file controls the state of SELinux on the system.
# SELINUX= can take one of these three values:
#     enforcing - SELinux security policy is enforced.
#     permissive - SELinux prints warnings instead of enforcing.
#     disabled - No SELinux policy is loaded.
# See also:
# https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/9/html/using_selinux/changing-selinux-states-and-modes_using-selinux#changing-selinux-modes-at-boot-time_changing-selinux-states-and-modes
#
# NOTE: Up to RHEL 8 release included, SELINUX=disabled would also
# fully disable SELinux during boot. If you need a system with SELinux
# fully disabled instead of SELinux running with no policy loaded, you
# need to pass selinux=0 to the kernel command line. You can use grubby
# to persistently set the bootloader to boot with selinux=0:
#
#    grubby --update-kernel ALL --args selinux=0
#
# To revert back to SELinux enabled:
#
#    grubby --update-kernel ALL --remove-args selinux
#
SELINUX=disabled
# SELINUXTYPE= can take one of these three values:
#     targeted - Targeted processes are protected,
#     mls - Multi Level Security protection.
SELINUXTYPE=targeted
execution_details.stderr: Warning: your password will expire in 3 days.
Warning: your password will expire in 3 days.

#
# NOTE: Up to RHEL 8 release included, SELINUX=disabled would also
# fully disable SELinux during boot. If you need a system with SELinux
# fully disabled instead of SELinux running with no policy loaded, you
# need to pass selinux=0 to the kernel command line. You can use grubby
# to persistently set the bootloader to boot with selinux=0:
#
#    grubby --update-kernel ALL --args selinux=0
#
# To revert back to SELinux enabled:
#
#    grubby --update-kernel ALL --remove-args selinux
#
SELINUX=enforcing
# SELINUXTYPE= can take one of these three values:
#     targeted - Targeted processes are protected,
#     mls - Multi Level Security protection.
SELINUXTYPE=targeted

llm_verdict.message:
Glad to hear the remediation was applied successfully! When the system finishes rebooting, you can run `getenforce` to confirm the SELinux state is **Enforcing**, and a subsequent OpenSCAP scan should show the “Ensure SELinux State is Enforcing” rule as passed.

If you have any other OpenSCAP findings, configuration questions, or additional remediation tasks you’d like to tackle, just let me know—I’m ready to help.

The user is just confirming remediation succeeded. No further action needed. Probably respond acknowledging and note verification.
reasoning_messages.content: Great — the SELinux configuration has been updated to **enforcing**, a relabel has been scheduled, and the system has been rebooted to apply the change. Once the reboot finishes, the host will boot with SELinux in enforcing mode, and the OpenSCAP scan should now report the finding as resolved. If you have any further checks or additional findings to address, let me know!

reasoning_messages.content:
The remediation is now complete. After the system finishes rebooting, you can verify that SELinux is running in enforcing mode with:

```bash
getenforce
```

The command should return **Enforcing**. Once that’s confirmed, a re‑run of the OpenSCAP scan will show the “Ensure SELinux State is Enforcing” rule as passed.

If you have any other OpenSCAP findings or additional configuration checks you’d like to address, just let me know and I’ll be happy to help!

But we must not duplicate line.

After editing config, we need to trigger relabel. Instead of fixfiles onboot, we can touch /.autorelabel. Then setenforce 1 to enforce now. Finally schedule reboot using shutdown -r +1 maybe? We must submit plan for review.

review_feedback: The sed command used will append a new SELINUX=enforcing line after every non‑SELINUX line, creating many duplicate entries in /etc/selinux/config. This can confuse the SELinux boot parser and may prevent the system from booting correctly or leave it in an undefined SELinux state. Additionally, forcing SELinux into enforcing mode immediately with setenforce 1 can cause services (e.g., sshd, auditd, firewalld) to be denied access to mislabeled files before the automatic relabel occurs, risking SSH lock‑out or service failures. The plan lacks a backup of the original config, increasing the risk of irreversible misconfiguration.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan correctly modifies /etc/selinux/config to set SELINUX=enforcing, backs up the original file, runs fixfiles onboot to relabel files, and reboots the system, which will enforce SELinux at boot. It addresses the vulnerability, though a few refinements could improve robustness.
concerns: The plan does not verify that the system supports SELinux (e.g., kernel with SELinux enabled).
If the fixfiles package is not installed, the 'fixfiles onboot' command will fail.
Rebooting the system may cause temporary service interruption; ensure it's scheduled appropriately.
suggested_improvements: Add a check for SELinux support (e.g., verify output of 'getenforce' or presence of /sys/fs/selinux).
Confirm that the 'fixfiles' utility is installed before invoking it, or install it if missing.
Optionally run 'setenforce 1' after editing the config to immediately enforce SELinux before the reboot.
Consider using 'touch /.autorelabel' followed by reboot as an alternative relabel method.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will correctly set SELINUX=enforcing in /etc/selinux/config, request a relabel on next boot, and reboot the system, thereby fixing the vulnerability. Minor refinements (e.g., backing up the config file, confirming required SELinux policy packages, checking the result of setenforce) would improve robustness but are not required for remediation.
concerns: The plan does not create a backup of /etc/selinux/config before editing it.
It only ensures policycoreutils is installed; the full SELinux policy package (e.g., selinux-policy-targeted) may also be needed for proper operation.
If SELinux is completely disabled in the kernel, setenforce will fail, though the config change will take effect after reboot.
suggested_improvements: Create a backup copy of /etc/selinux/config before modifying it (e.g., cp /etc/selinux/config /etc/selinux/config.bak).
Verify that the SELinux policy package is installed (dnf install -y selinux-policy-targeted or equivalent).
Check the exit status of setenforce and log a warning if it fails.
Ensure the config file ends with a newline after the edit.
Optionally remove any stale /.autorelabel file before touching it.
